chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Time period: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)) where I is about the pivot and d is pivot-to-CM distance
Equivalent simple pendulum length: L_eq = I/(md)
Using parallel axis theorem: I = I_cm + md², so T = 2π√((I_cm + md²)/(mgd))
For a uniform rod pivoted at end: T = 2π√(2L/(3g))
T has a minimum at d = √(I_cm/m) — neither too close nor too far from CM
A simple pendulum is a special case where all mass is at one point: I = md², T = 2π√(d/g)
truth_statements: A physical pendulum is any rigid body oscillating about a horizontal axis that does not pass through its centre of mass
The time period depends on the moment of inertia about the pivot and the distance from the pivot to the centre of mass
The equivalent length of a physical pendulum is always greater than the distance from pivot to CM
There exists an optimal pivot distance that minimises the time period
The simple pendulum formula T = 2π√(L/g) is a special case of the physical pendulum formula

aha_moment.what_to_show: A ruler pivoted at different points along its length. Near the end: long T (large I). Near the centre: long T (small d, small restoring torque). At an intermediate point: minimum T. The T vs d curve shows a clear minimum.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Graph of T vs pivot position with the minimum clearly marked, alongside the ruler swinging at each position
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume pivot closer to CM always gives faster oscillation — seeing T increase again near the CM (due to vanishing restoring torque) is counterintuitive and memorable

simulation_states.label: Physical vs simple pendulum
simulation_states.description: A rod swinging alongside a simple pendulum. Different periods — the rod has distributed mass so it oscillates differently. Formula comparison shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: A rod is NOT a simple pendulum — mass distribution matters

simulation_states.label: Varying pivot position
simulation_states.description: The pivot point moves along a uniform rod. At each position, the rod swings and T is recorded. Far from CM: large I dominates → long T. Close to CM: small d → weak restoring torque → long T.
simulation_states.emphasis: T is long at both extremes — there's a sweet spot in between

simulation_states.label: Minimum period
simulation_states.description: Graph of T vs d shows a clear minimum at d = √(I_cm/m). The rod swings at this optimal position. Equivalent simple pendulum length L_eq = I/(md) is shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: The minimum T occurs at d = √(I_cm/m), not at the end or centre

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student drags the pivot point along a uniform rod. The rod swings and T is measured. A graph builds up showing T vs pivot position. Compare with simple pendulum of same total length.
simulation_states.emphasis: Drag the pivot — find the position with shortest period

common_misconceptions.belief: A physical pendulum is just a simple pendulum with a heavy bob
common_misconceptions.correction: A physical pendulum has distributed mass with moment of inertia I about the pivot, not concentrated at a point. Its period T = 2π√(I/(mgd)) involves I, not just the distance d.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Swing a rod and a simple pendulum side by side — different periods for same pivot-to-CM distance

common_misconceptions.belief: Moving the pivot closer to the CM always makes it oscillate faster
common_misconceptions.correction: Moving closer to CM reduces the restoring torque (τ = mgd sinθ, d decreases). Eventually T increases again. There's an optimal d = √(I_cm/m) that minimises T.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show T increasing as pivot approaches CM — the minimum is NOT at the CM
